content.text: When building agentic systems, one of the most consequential architectural decisions is whether the agent loop — the code that calls the LLM, dispatches tools, and assembles results — runs synchronously or asynchronously. The choice affects throughput, resource usage, code complexity, and how many concurrent sessions a single process can serve. Because agents spend the majority of their wall-clock time waiting on network I/O (LLM APIs, databases, external services), this decision matters far more for agents than for typical CRUD applications.

means that each operation runs to completion before the next one starts. When a sync function makes an HTTP request, the thread blocks — it sits idle — until the response arrives. The operating system can schedule other threads during this wait, but the calling thread itself is stalled.
means that I/O operations are started but do not block the current thread. Instead, the program registers a callback or uses an
expression to pause the current coroutine, returning control to an event loop that can run other coroutines. When the I/O completes, the event loop resumes the paused coroutine.

The key distinction is not speed per operation — a single HTTP call takes the same wall-clock time whether it is sync or async. The difference is what happens while the call is in flight. In a sync model, the thread waits. In an async model, the event loop can service other coroutines during that wait, dramatically increasing the number of concurrent operations a single thread can handle.
In Python, async execution is built around the
syntax introduced in Python 3.5. In JavaScript, async execution is built into the runtime via the Promises API and the
syntax standardized in ES2017. Both languages use a single-threaded event loop that multiplexes many concurrent coroutines.

Agents are dominated by I/O latency. A typical agent turn might: send a prompt to an LLM API and wait 1–4 seconds; call three different tool endpoints in sequence and wait 200–800 ms each; persist state to a database; and stream the result back to the caller. Under a sync model, all of these waits are additive. The thread is blocked and cannot serve any other user session during this time.

For a production agent serving many users concurrently, the sync model requires a separate thread or process per concurrent session. Threads are not free: each consumes memory (typically 1–8 MB stack on Linux), and context-switching between hundreds of threads adds CPU overhead. With long-running agent turns (seconds to minutes), the cost of the thread-per-session model grows quickly.

The async model addresses this by allowing one thread to interleave many in-flight I/O operations. A single-process async agent server can handle hundreds or even thousands of concurrent turns that are each waiting on network responses, using far less memory and CPU than an equivalent multi-threaded sync server would require.

Beyond raw concurrency, async also enables parallelizing independent tool calls within a single agent turn. If an agent needs to call a search API and a weather API independently, async allows both calls to be in-flight simultaneously, reducing the turn's total latency by the duration of the shorter call.
High concurrency at low memory cost
— one event-loop thread can handle hundreds of concurrent I/O-waiting agent turns without allocating a thread stack per session.
Parallel tool calls within a turn
(JS) dispatches independent tool calls simultaneously, reducing turn latency by the overlap.
— async servers have more predictable memory usage under load than thread-per-request servers, because they do not allocate new threads per connection.
— modern agent frameworks (FastAPI, Starlette, LangChain async, Anthropic SDK async client) are built around async, so async code integrates cleanly with streaming responses, SSE, and WebSockets.
— for scripts, notebooks, and batch jobs that do not need concurrency, sync code is easier to read, debug, trace, and test. Stack traces are linear; there is no event loop to reason about.
Async code is not universally superior. The right choice depends on the deployment context.
Async propagates through the call stack

— once a function is async, every caller must also be async. Mixing sync and async code carelessly causes subtle bugs (e.g., calling a sync blocking function inside an async context freezes the entire event loop).

— async stack traces are fragmented across event loop iterations. Exceptions inside coroutines can be silently swallowed if tasks are not awaited properly. Tools like asyncio debug mode help but add overhead.

— async's concurrency gains only apply to I/O-bound waiting. If a tool runs a heavy embedding computation or a large JSON parse, awaiting it does not help; it still blocks the event loop on the CPU. CPU-bound work must be offloaded to a thread pool (
— sync servers need a thread or process per concurrent session. Each thread consumes memory; context-switching adds CPU cost. Under high concurrency, sync approaches hit OS limits.
— not all third-party libraries have async clients. Using a sync-only library inside an async server requires wrapping calls in a thread executor, adding boilerplate.
— for a script that runs one agent turn at a time, async adds complexity with no benefit. Start sync; migrate to async when concurrent load demands it.
event loop is a scheduler that runs in a single thread. It maintains a queue of ready-to-run coroutines. When a coroutine reaches an

expression on an I/O operation, it suspends itself — registering a callback for when the I/O completes — and returns control to the event loop. The event loop picks the next ready coroutine and runs it until it either finishes or hits another
This cooperative multitasking means coroutines must voluntarily yield at
points. A coroutine that runs a tight CPU loop for 500 ms without hitting an
will block all other coroutines for that 500 ms — this is called blocking the event loop.
. Calling it does not execute it; it returns a coroutine object. To actually run it, either
it inside another coroutine or submit it to the event loop with
. Coroutines are lightweight — they are Python objects, not OS threads — so creating thousands of them has negligible overhead.

is scheduled by the kernel. Python's Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) means threads do not run Python bytecode in parallel, but they do release the GIL during I/O system calls, so multiple threads can have I/O in flight simultaneously. For I/O-bound workloads, threads work — but they consume more memory and have higher context-switch cost than coroutines.

schedules all the given coroutines as concurrent tasks and waits until all complete, returning a list of results in the same order. If any task raises an exception, gather re-raises it by default. For fire-and-forget tasks use
, but always keep a reference to the task object to prevent it from being garbage-collected before completion.
The diagram below contrasts a sync agent turn (left) with an async agent turn (right) for the same set of operations: one LLM call followed by three independent tool calls.

attrs.alt: Sync vs Async agent turn execution timeline

content.text: In the sync model, each operation runs sequentially: the LLM call (≈2 s) completes, then Tool A (≈0.5 s), Tool B (≈0.5 s), and Tool C (≈0.5 s) run one after another, totalling ≈3.6 s per turn. In the async model, the LLM call is awaited (the event loop runs other sessions during this wait), then all three tool calls are dispatched concurrently with asyncio.gather, completing in ≈0.5 s (the max of the three), totalling ≈2.6 s. Each additional concurrent session using async does not block others — they share the same event loop and interleave their waits.
The agent server handles many concurrent sessions (tens or more) that each involve multiple I/O-bound waits.
The deployment uses a production ASGI framework such as FastAPI or Starlette, which are built on async.
The agent turn involves multiple independent tool calls that can be parallelized.
The agent streams responses (SSE, WebSocket) — streaming requires an async-compatible web server.
Memory efficiency matters: async enables many concurrent turns in one process without one thread per turn.
The use case is a CLI tool, notebook, or batch script — single-user, no concurrency requirement.
The team is unfamiliar with async patterns and the deployment will handle low concurrency (e.g., a small internal tool with <10 concurrent users where a thread pool suffices).
The agent relies on a blocking third-party SDK with no async support and wrapping it in an executor is not practical.
Debugging and observability tooling at the organization does not yet support async traces well.
A specific tool performs CPU-heavy computation (embedding, tokenization, parsing). Use
to offload it to a thread pool inside an async server.
The entire agent workload is CPU-bound rather than I/O-bound (rare for LLM agents). Use multiprocessing.
The agent must call a legacy blocking SDK that cannot be wrapped easily — a dedicated thread pool isolates the blocking call.
Setting up an async agent loop in Python
The entry point for an async agent in Python is
, which creates the event loop and runs a coroutine to completion. Inside async functions, use

import asyncio
import httpx  # async-native HTTP client

async def call_llm(client: httpx.AsyncClient, messages: list) -> dict:
    """Call a hypothetical LLM API endpoint."""
    response = await client.post(
        "https://api.example.com/v1/chat",
        json={"messages": messages},
        timeout=30.0,
    )
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()

async def run_tool(client: httpx.AsyncClient, tool_name: str, args: dict) -> dict:
    """Dispatch a single tool call and return the result."""
    response = await client.post(
        f"https://tools.example.com/{tool_name}",
        json=args,
        timeout=15.0,
    )
    response.raise_for_status()
    return {"tool": tool_name, "result": response.json()}

async def agent_turn(messages: list) -> list:
    """One full agent turn: LLM call then parallel tool dispatch."""
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        # Await LLM — event loop serves other sessions during the wait
        llm_response = await call_llm(client, messages)
        tool_calls = llm_response.get("tool_calls", [])

        if tool_calls:
            # Dispatch all tool calls concurrently
            tool_tasks = [
                run_tool(client, tc["name"], tc["arguments"])
                for tc in tool_calls
            ]
            tool_results = await asyncio.gather(*tool_tasks)
            messages.extend(tool_results)

        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": llm_response["content"]})
        return messages

if __name__ == "__main__":
    history = [{"role": "user", "content": "Summarise today's weather and news."}]
    result = asyncio.run(agent_turn(history))
    print(result[-1]["content"])
For a CLI agent or a simple script, sync code is perfectly appropriate. The Anthropic Python SDK provides both sync and async clients:

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()  # sync client

def agent_turn_sync(messages: list) -> str:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-opus-4-5",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=messages,
    )
    return response.content[0].text

# Simple script: no asyncio needed
reply = agent_turn_sync([{"role": "user", "content": "List three Python async tips."}])
print(reply)
Offloading blocking code from an async server
When an async server must call a synchronous blocking function (e.g., a legacy SDK), use
to run it in a thread pool without blocking the event loop:

import asyncio
import time

def slow_sync_tool(query: str) -> str:
    """Hypothetical blocking SDK call."""
    time.sleep(0.5)  # simulates network I/O
    return f"result for: {query}"

async def async_wrapper(query: str) -> str:
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    # Run the blocking function in the default thread-pool executor
    result = await loop.run_in_executor(None, slow_sync_tool, query)
    return result

async def main():
    # Both calls run concurrently even though slow_sync_tool is blocking
    r1, r2 = await asyncio.gather(
        async_wrapper("question one"),
        async_wrapper("question two"),
    )
    print(r1, r2)

asyncio.run(main())
Example 1 — measuring sync vs async tool latency (Python)
This runnable script measures the wall-clock difference between sequential sync tool calls and concurrent async tool calls using asyncio.sleep to simulate I/O latency:

import asyncio
import time

# --- Sync approach ---
def fake_tool_sync(name: str, latency: float) -> str:
    time.sleep(latency)
    return f"{name}: done"

def run_sync_tools():
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    results = [
        fake_tool_sync("search",  0.6),
        fake_tool_sync("weather", 0.4),
        fake_tool_sync("calendar",0.5),
    ]
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
    print(f"Sync:  {elapsed:.2f}s  results={results}")

# --- Async approach ---
async def fake_tool_async(name: str, latency: float) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(latency)
    return f"{name}: done"

async def run_async_tools():
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        fake_tool_async("search",   0.6),
        fake_tool_async("weather",  0.4),
        fake_tool_async("calendar", 0.5),
    )
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
    print(f"Async: {elapsed:.2f}s  results={list(results)}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    run_sync_tools()    # ≈ 1.5 s  (0.6 + 0.4 + 0.5)
    asyncio.run(run_async_tools())  # ≈ 0.6 s  (max of three)
Example 2 — async agent server with FastAPI
A minimal FastAPI agent endpoint that serves multiple concurrent sessions asynchronously, each awaiting an LLM response without blocking other requests:

# pip install fastapi uvicorn httpx anthropic
import asyncio
import anthropic
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel

app = FastAPI()
aclient = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()  # async Anthropic client

class TurnRequest(BaseModel):
    messages: list[dict]
    model: str = "claude-haiku-4-5"
    max_tokens: int = 512

@app.post("/agent/turn")
async def agent_turn(req: TurnRequest) -> dict:
    """
    Each request awaits the LLM independently.
    FastAPI runs this in the event loop, so many requests
    are handled concurrently without one blocking another.
    """
    response = await aclient.messages.create(
        model=req.model,
        max_tokens=req.max_tokens,
        messages=req.messages,
    )
    return {
        "reply": response.content[0].text,
        "usage": {
            "input_tokens":  response.usage.input_tokens,
            "output_tokens": response.usage.output_tokens,
        },
    }

# Run with: uvicorn solution:app --workers 1
# One worker, one event loop, many concurrent turns

Consider a customer support agent platform that serves 500 simultaneous users, each in the middle of a multi-turn conversation. Each turn involves: calling an LLM (≈2 s average), querying a CRM API (≈300 ms), and checking an order database (≈200 ms).

Under a sync model with a thread pool of 100 threads (a generous allocation), at most 100 turns are active simultaneously. With an average turn duration of 2.5 s, throughput peaks at 40 turns/second. To serve 500 concurrent users each expecting sub-3-second responses, the platform would need 500 threads or 5 separate processes, consuming GBs of memory.

Under an async model, a single process with one event loop handles all 500 concurrent turns. While each turn awaits the LLM, the loop services other coroutines. The CRM and database calls within each turn are dispatched concurrently with asyncio.gather, cutting the per-turn latency from 2.5 s (sequential) to 2.3 s (CRM and DB overlap). Total memory footprint is a fraction of the thread-based model.
The production architecture for this platform uses:
— async HTTP calls to LLM and CRM APIs.
— async PostgreSQL driver for the order database.
— parallel dispatch of CRM and DB queries within each turn.
— wrapping a legacy sync PDF-parsing library that cannot be replaced.
One async worker handles 500 concurrent users. The same load required 5 sync worker processes previously — reducing infrastructure cost by 80%.
Remaining CPU bottleneck: embedding generation for retrieval-augmented context. This is offloaded to a dedicated worker pool, not the event loop.
Mistake 1 — blocking the event loop with CPU-heavy code
Calling a synchronous CPU-bound function inside an async function without a thread executor blocks every other coroutine for the duration:

import asyncio, time

# WRONG: blocks the event loop
async def embed_text_bad(text: str) -> list:
    # Imagine this takes 300 ms of CPU
    return compute_embedding_sync(text)  # blocks all other coroutines!

# RIGHT: offload to thread executor
async def embed_text_good(text: str) -> list:
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    return await loop.run_in_executor(None, compute_embedding_sync, text)
Mistake 2 — forgetting to await a coroutine
returns a coroutine object instead of executing it. Python 3.x emits a
but does not raise an error, so the mistake can go unnoticed:

# WRONG: result is a coroutine object, not the actual reply
result = call_llm(messages)   # no await — bug!
print(result)  # prints: <coroutine object call_llm at 0x...>

# RIGHT:
result = await call_llm(messages)
print(result)  # prints the actual response
Mistake 3 — mixing sync and async clients
Using a synchronous SDK client inside an async function works (the call will block the event loop), but it defeats the purpose of async. Always use the async variant of the SDK client when available:

import anthropic

# WRONG inside an async server — blocks event loop during LLM call
sync_client = anthropic.Anthropic()
async def bad_turn(messages):
    return sync_client.messages.create(...)  # blocking!

# RIGHT — use the async client
async_client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic()
async def good_turn(messages):
    return await async_client.messages.create(...)

, exceptions inside the task are not raised immediately — they surface only when the task is awaited or when it is garbage-collected (printing an error to stderr). Always await tasks or handle exceptions explicitly:

async def risky():
    raise ValueError("tool error")

# WRONG: exception is silently swallowed until GC
task = asyncio.create_task(risky())
# ... forgetting to await task

# RIGHT: always await or catch
try:
    task = asyncio.create_task(risky())
    await task
except ValueError as e:
    print(f"Tool failed: {e}")
Start sync, migrate to async when concurrency demands it
— premature async adds complexity. Measure actual concurrent load before committing to async architecture.
— any function that blocks the CPU for more than ≈5 ms should be offloaded to a thread executor. Profile with
debug mode to detect blocking calls.
Prefer asyncio.gather for parallel tool calls
— it is idiomatic, handles exceptions cleanly with return_exceptions=True for partial failures, and makes parallelism explicit.
Set timeouts on every I/O operation
— LLM APIs and external tools can hang. Use
or the client-level timeout parameter to prevent coroutines from waiting indefinitely.
to ensure all tasks are awaited and exceptions are not silently dropped.
— async code interleaves log lines from different sessions. Attach a per-session correlation ID using
so logs can be filtered per session in production.

The concurrency advantage of async is most pronounced when session count is high and individual I/O waits are long (network latency dominates). In these conditions, the performance ratio between async and sync approaches the ratio of average I/O wait time to total turn time.

For example, if a turn takes 3 s and 2.8 s is I/O wait, one sync thread handles at most 1/3 ≈ 0.33 turns/s. An async event loop servicing all the I/O concurrently can handle many more sessions on the same core, limited by CPU time only (≈0.2 s per turn on CPU) — theoretically 5 turns/s per core. In practice, gains of 10–50x in concurrent sessions per process are typical for I/O-heavy agents.
Use wrk, locust, or k6 to benchmark concurrent agent throughput under realistic I/O latency distributions.
Measure p95 and p99 latencies, not just means — async systems can have better median latency but worse tail latency if the event loop is occasionally starved by a long-running coroutine (
to detect callbacks that exceed your threshold.

Async enables one process to handle many sessions (vertical scaling within a process). It does not replace horizontal scaling — deploy multiple async workers behind a load balancer for resilience and additional throughput.
For CPU-bound workloads (embedding, reranking), async alone is insufficient — use separate GPU/CPU workers and task queues (Celery, ARQ, Dramatiq) to offload heavy compute from the event loop.
— standard library event loop; the foundation for everything else.
— async HTTP client; drop-in complement to the sync requests library. Use
— official Anthropic async client; same API as the sync client but returns coroutines.
— async ASGI web frameworks; async handler functions are first-class.
— async database drivers for PostgreSQL and MySQL.
— async Redis client for state storage and pub/sub in agent pipelines.
— compatibility layer that abstracts asyncio and trio event loops; used internally by httpx and Starlette.
— built into Node.js; no external library needed.
— official TypeScript/JS SDK; all methods return Promises and support async/await.
— async HTTP clients; undici is the modern choice with fetch-compatible API.
— Express 5 supports async route handlers natively; Express 4 requires a wrapper to catch async errors.

import asyncio

# Enable debug mode: logs slow callbacks and unawaited coroutines
asyncio.run(main(), debug=True)

# Or set environment variable before running:
# PYTHONASYNCIODEBUG=1 python agent.py
Async code makes it easy to forget timeouts. An LLM API that takes 60 seconds instead of 2 will hold a coroutine and its memory for that duration. Always set timeouts: use
to the HTTP client. Without timeouts, a slow external API can exhaust the coroutine pool and degrade the entire service.
When a client disconnects (HTTP connection closed), FastAPI cancels the corresponding coroutine with an
. Ensure that cleanup code (releasing database connections, cancelling downstream requests) is inside a

async def agent_turn_safe():
    conn = await db_pool.acquire()
    try:
        result = await run_agent(conn)
        return result
    finally:
        # Runs even if the coroutine is cancelled
        await db_pool.release(conn)
Async code shares mutable state within a single event loop iteration without preemption, so race conditions are less common than with threads. However, they are still possible across
points. If two coroutines read-modify-write shared state and each has an
in between, the invariant can be violated. Use
to guard shared mutable state across await boundaries.
raises the first exception and cancels remaining tasks. For agent tool calls where partial success is acceptable, use
instances rather than letting the entire turn fail because one tool errored:

A useful analogy: imagine a chef (the event loop) managing multiple cooking stations (coroutines). A sync chef completes one dish from start to finish before starting the next — if the pasta needs 10 minutes to boil, the chef stands at the pot the entire time. An async chef starts the pasta boiling, then moves to prep the salad, then checks on the sauce — always doing useful work whenever something is waiting. The async chef does not cook any individual component faster, but the overall kitchen throughput is much higher.

The critical implication: if the chef tries to chop onions (CPU work) while the pasta is boiling, the chopping takes their full attention and they cannot check the sauce (the event loop is blocked). Heavy CPU work must be delegated to a sous chef (thread or process pool).
Async does not make individual I/O calls faster; it improves throughput by overlapping many concurrent waits on a single thread.
An agent loop is almost always I/O-bound (LLM calls, tool APIs, databases), making it an excellent candidate for async when serving multiple concurrent sessions.
to dispatch independent tool calls in parallel within a single agent turn — this directly reduces per-turn latency.
Never call blocking I/O or CPU-heavy functions directly inside an async function — use
to offload them to a thread pool.
Sync code is simpler to debug and is the right choice for CLI agents, notebooks, and low-concurrency batch scripts; async is right for high-concurrency API servers.
Always set timeouts on every awaitable I/O call (
or client-level timeout) to prevent slow external services from holding coroutines indefinitely.
Use the async variant of SDKs and database drivers throughout the stack; mixing sync blocking clients into an async server defeats the purpose and blocks the event loop.
to prevent subtle async-specific race conditions.
Profile before migrating: measure actual concurrent session count and I/O wait fraction — async's benefits are large when many sessions overlap I/O waits, and negligible when concurrency is low.
